geometry.rationale: Figure 1 maintains the correct spatial relationship with Panel A on the left and Panel B on the right, whereas Figure 2's camera azimuth of 325 degrees views the scene from behind, incorrectly mirroring the layout and placing Panel A on the right.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 correctly places labels like (0,0,0) at the proper origin point and keeps text legible, whereas Figure 1 places (0,0,0) on the wrong side, has confusing label placement, and allows the 'Pitch' label to clash illegibly with its rotation curve.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 dynamically scales and positions the image-plane coordinate arrows (and their labels) to exactly measure the projected point 'p' as it moves, directly illustrating the decomposition into (x,y) coordinates, whereas Figure 1 just uses static axis arrows.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 2 is the clear winner because it offers superior interactivity with real-time numerical slider readouts and dynamic visual elements that dynamically scale to measure projected coordinates, whereas Figure 1 relies on static text popups and rigid axis arrows. Additionally, Figure 2 maintains a clearer left-to-right compositional flow with legible, accurately placed labels, avoiding the confusing layout and overlapping text present in Figure 1.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 perfectly constrains the 2D projected velocity vector to lie flat on the image plane at z=f, whereas Figure 2 applies an uneven Z-offset to prevent z-fighting that incorrectly tilts the 2D optical flow vector out of the image plane.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 features a direct-manipulation drag interaction allowing the user to freely drag the tip of the 3D velocity vector in space, whereas Figure 2 limits velocity control to a simple 1D angle slider.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 features accurately sized serif labels that match the original typography and correctly aligns the projected velocity vector with the dashed sightline, whereas Figure 1 suffers from illegibly small labels, poorly proportioned arrowheads, and fails to align the projected vector geometrically.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 features significantly better label size and styling that match the original, and correctly differentiates the uppercase 'P' dot from the lowercase 'p' dot, whereas Figure 1 has tiny, hard-to-read labels and incorrectly uses a lowercase 'p' dot for the 3D point.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 allows freeform direct manipulation of the 3D velocity vector, providing a much richer exploration of optical flow projection, whereas Figure 2 restricts the velocity direction to a slider-controlled rotation with a hardcoded vertical component that contradicts its own 'pure depth motion' explanation.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a significantly richer and mathematically accurate interactive experience by allowing freeform 3D manipulation of the velocity vector without distorting its 2D projection on the image plane, whereas Figure 2 restricts interaction to a 1D slider and introduces an incorrect geometric tilt to the projected vector. While Figure 2 features much better typographic faithfulness and label legibility compared to the tiny, poorly proportioned text in Figure 1, these presentation flaws are outweighed by Figure 1's superior conceptual execution and interactivity.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 uses the correct mathematical formulas (e.g., the exact Fourier transform of the 5-point Laplacian) to generate geometrically accurate frequency response surfaces, whereas Figure 2 hallucinates arbitrary trigonometric functions to fake the shapes.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 includes a developer-built control panel with a dropdown and slider, as well as an animated step-through interaction upon clicking, whereas Figure 2 only features basic click-to-show-text tooltips with no UI controls.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 accurately replicates the original's axis styling, italicized labels, and specific grid rendering (especially for plot c), whereas Figure 1 uses overly thick axis lines and includes distracting UI elements not present in the reference.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 features readable, italicized 'u' and 'v' axis labels that closely match the original's style, whereas Figure 1 uses very small, poorly placed labels and adds an unreferenced 'Magnitude' label.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 uses the exact mathematical formulas for the continuous LoG and discrete 5-point Laplacian frequency responses, providing real pedagogical value, whereas Figure 2 hallucinates arbitrary polynomials and trigonometric functions to visually fake the surfaces.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 is the stronger choice because it prioritizes mathematical accuracy by using the exact continuous and discrete frequency response formulas, paired with a robust interactive UI panel. Although Figure 2 more closely replicates the original aesthetic styling and italicized labels, it critically fails by hallucinating arbitrary trigonometric functions to fake the surface geometries and offering only minimal tooltip interactions.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 accurately reconstructs the full 3D bounding cage, tick marks, and axis layout characteristic of a textbook 3D stem plot, whereas Figure 1 uses a generic grid and omits the standard plot bounding box.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers superior interactivity by allowing users to dynamically adjust the filter order and toggle normalization to generate new geometries and update the mathematical matrix in real time, whereas Figure 2 only provides a simple dropdown to toggle the visibility of pre-built, static scenes.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 better replicates the original's composition by placing the vertical axis and its labels (0 to 4) on the left side, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly centers the vertical axis and uses a much darker, less accurate shade of blue.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 correctly positions the horizontal z-axis label on the left with all tick values (including 4), whereas Figure 2 awkwardly rotates it along the central axis and omits the top tick value.

concept.rationale: Figure 2 goes beyond a simple spatial plot by visually explaining the filter's separable construction (outer product) and its low-pass frequency response, which are the fundamental theoretical concepts for understanding its computational efficiency and smoothing behavior, whereas Figure 1 only explores changing the filter size.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides superior interactivity by allowing dynamic updates to geometries and mathematical matrices via parameter controls, whereas Figure 2 limits users to toggling between pre-built static scenes. Furthermore, Figure 1 correctly places the axes and includes all tick values, whereas Figure 2 awkwardly centers the vertical axis, rotates horizontal labels, and uses an inaccurate shade of blue. Although Figure 2 captures the 3D bounding cage and underlying mathematical concepts more effectively, Figure 1's superior interactive capabilities and precise faithful labeling secure its overall advantage.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 includes the critical 3D untangling manifold ribbon that flattens as it moves upward, whereas Figure 2 entirely omits this key geometric element.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 offers more pedagogically meaningful interactions, notably a View Mode toggle that seamlessly switches between discrete paths and a continuous untangling manifold, along with a speed control, whereas Figure 2's layer highlighting is less visually informative.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 more accurately replicates the curved trajectories and fills all horizontal planes, whereas Figure 2 incorrectly uses straight paths, only fills the bottom plane, completely omits the side text labels, and adds uncharacteristic overshooting lines to the box geometry.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 includes all four text labels from the original, whereas Figure 2 completely omits the side labels for 'Representation learning' and 'Generative modeling'.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 includes a 'Manifold Unfolding' view that explicitly illustrates how deep layers untangle a twisted data manifold into a flat representation space, which is a crucial pedagogical concept missing from Figure 2.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 strongly outperforms Figure 2 by accurately incorporating the critical 3D untangling manifold ribbon and curved trajectories, whereas Figure 2 uses incorrect straight paths and completely omits the key manifold element. Additionally, Figure 1 faithfully includes all text labels and offers more meaningful interactions like a view mode toggle, while Figure 2 is missing essential side labels and relies on less informative layer highlighting.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly adjusts the true 3D heights of the stems relative to a fixed bounding box when normalized, whereas Figure 2 auto-scales the visual height to be nearly constant, breaking the geometric representation of the data values.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides a noticeably better interactive experience by implementing smooth height animations (tweening) and dynamic camera refitting when parameters change, whereas Figure 2 instantaneously snaps between states.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 2 correctly displays the 3D stem plot with blue spheres and lines matching the reference's composition, whereas Figure 1 is rendered too small and fails to visibly show the data points.

labels.rationale: Figure 2 features readable, correctly placed labels for axes and ticks that closely match the original, whereas Figure 1's labels are illegibly small and impossible to read.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 correctly scales the visual height of the normalized coefficients as the filter support expands (showing that the height must decrease to maintain a sum of 1), whereas Figure 2 dynamically rescales the axis so normalized coefficients always appear at the same visual height regardless of filter size, undermining the core concept.

aggregator.explanation: Although Figure 2 boasts superior visual faithfulness and highly readable labels compared to Figure 1's illegibly small rendering, Figure 1 is the overall winner because it accurately preserves the data's core mathematical concept. Specifically, Figure 1 correctly adjusts the 3D heights of the stems as the filter support expands, whereas Figure 2 improperly auto-scales the visual height to remain constant, thereby undermining the geometric representation of the data. Furthermore, Figure 1 offers a better interactive experience with smooth animations, unlike Figure 2's abrupt snapping between states.

geometry.rationale: Figure 1 correctly sets the initial camera azimuth to view the stereo setup from the front, clearly showing the baseline and both image planes, whereas Figure 2 sets the azimuth to view along the X-axis (baseline), obscuring the spatial relationship between the cameras.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 provides a more comprehensive set of interactive 3D elements with hover tooltips and popups (17 interactive objects compared to 8 in Figure 2), includes native HTML tooltips on the UI sliders, and features a more intuitive progressive step-through animation tied to a single object click.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the layout, colors, and key geometric elements of the original diagram, whereas Figure 2 suffers from a broken perspective that collapses the scene and fails to render the yellow epipolar plane.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 maintains readable, appropriately sized labels that are distributed across the scene, whereas Figure 2 suffers from severe label overlap and clutter that renders most text illegible.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 correctly implements the camera framing and provides a cleaner geometric representation, whereas Figure 2 suffers from a misleading description stating that moving the point laterally rotates the epipolar plane (which is mathematically false given the baseline setup), and its epipolar lines frequently clip poorly or jump out of the plane boundaries.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a significantly better representation of the stereo camera setup by correctly setting the camera angle to show the baseline and image planes, whereas Figure 2 suffers from a broken perspective that obscures these spatial relationships. Additionally, Figure 1 features readable labels, robust interactivity, and accurate conceptual geometry, whereas Figure 2 is hindered by illegible overlapping text and a misleading mathematical description of the epipolar plane.

geometry.rationale: Figure 2 constructs a genuine 3D spatial relationship for the camera geometries and image planes, whereas Figure 1 improperly draws a flat 2D diagram onto a single 3D plane, violating the prompt's explicit prohibition of 2D canvases in 3D space.

interactivity.rationale: Figure 1 features a highly intuitive direct 3D manipulation interaction where the user can drag the 3D point directly on the canvas to change its depth along the ray, whereas Figure 2 relies purely on standard sliders and contains a critical ReferenceError (missing updateLabels function) that breaks its interactivity.

faithfulness.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the layout, red point colors, and curved transformation arrows of the original, whereas Figure 2 suffers from broken, overlapping geometry, missing point colors, and incorrect text placement.

labels.rationale: Figure 1 accurately reproduces the text, size, and placement of all labels from the original, whereas Figure 2's labels are extremely small, cluttered, and difficult to read.

concept.rationale: Figure 1 successfully visualizes the conceptual relationship between depth and projection (albeit using simplified 2D interpolation), whereas Figure 2 completely fails to run due to a runtime ReferenceError from a missing updateLabels function, conveying nothing to the student.

aggregator.explanation: Figure 1 provides a functional, intuitive visualization of depth projection with accurate labels and direct 3D manipulation, whereas Figure 2 suffers from a runtime error that completely breaks its interactivity and conceptual delivery. Although Figure 2 attempts a genuine 3D spatial construction unlike Figure 1's improper 2D diagram, Figure 2's broken execution, overlapping geometry, and illegible labels make it far less effective overall.
